coredata.dc:title: Molecularly imprinted polymer containing fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a new fluorescent nanosensor for detection of methamphetamine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Masteri-Farahani, Majid
Mashhadi-Ramezani, Shadi
Mosleh, Nazanin
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent nanosensor based on graphene quantum dots embedded within molecularly imprinted polymer (GQDs@MIP) was developed for detection and determination of methamphetamine (METH). The resulting GQDs@MIP nanocomposite exhibited higher methamphetamine selectivity in comparison with corresponding non-imprinted polymer (GQDs@NIP). Characterization of the GQDs@MIP nanocomposite was done by nitrogen adsorption and desorption analysis (BET method), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), photoluminescence (PL), ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis), and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopies. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs@MIP was efficiently quenched in the presence of methamphetamine template molecules while no quenching was observed in the presence of other analytes such as amphetamine, ibuprofen, codeine, and morphine. Using this method, the detection limit of 1.7 μg/L was obtained for methamphetamine determination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Nanosensor
Methamphetamine
Fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of rGQDs (a), vGQDs (b), GQDs@MIP after (c), and before (d) template removing.
(Top) TEM image of GQDs@MIP, (Bottom) Nitrogen sorption isotherms of free GQDs@MIP before and after addition of METH.
UV–Vis spectra of (a) GQDs, (b) rGQDs, and (c) vGQDs.

Fluorescence spectra of (a) GQDs@NIP, (b) and (c) GQDs@MIP before and after template extraction, and (d) template free GQDs@MIP after addition of 50 μM of METH. The concentration of GQDs@MIP and GQDs@NIP was 2 mg.mL− 1.

Fluorescence spectra of (a) GQDs@NIP and (b) GQDs@MIP upon addition of various amount of (0 to 50 μM with intervals of 10 μM) METH. (c) Related Stern-Volmer plots from GQDs@MIP and GQDs@NIP with METH. The concentration of GQDs@MIP and GQDs@NIP was 2 mg/mL.
The fluorescence response of MIP sensor toward the various amount of (0 to 50 μM with intervals of 10 μM) (a) AMPH, (b) IBU, (c) COD, (d) MORPH.
Comparison of the Stern-Volmer quenching constants of GQDs@MIP and GQDs@NIP toward the METH, AMPH, IBU, COD, and MORPH.
Fluorescence quenching of GQDs@MIP in the presence of methamphetamine.
Analytical parameters for detection of METH by different methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Solid-phase microextraction/liquid chromatography/electrospray ionization/tandem mass spectrometry.

head.ce:title: Molecularly imprinted polymer containing fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a new fluorescent nanosensor for detection of methamphetamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Islamic Republic of Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Islamic Republic of Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent nanosensor based on graphene quantum dots embedded within molecularly imprinted polymer (GQDs@MIP) was developed for detection and determination of methamphetamine (METH). The resulting GQDs@MIP nanocomposite exhibited higher methamphetamine selectivity in comparison with corresponding non-imprinted polymer (GQDs@NIP). Characterization of the GQDs@MIP nanocomposite was done by nitrogen adsorption and desorption analysis (BET method), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), photoluminescence (PL), ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis), and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopies. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs@MIP was efficiently quenched in the presence of methamphetamine template molecules while no quenching was observed in the presence of other analytes such as amphetamine, ibuprofen, codeine, and morphine. Using this method, the detection limit of 1.7 μg/L was obtained for methamphetamine determination.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials play significant roles in designing various chemical sensors and biosensors [1–3]. Quantum dots (QDs) as an important class of fluorescent nanomaterials have been utilized in designing optical sensors for highly sensitive measurement of various analytes [4–7]. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possessing good water dispersibility, tunable fluorescence properties, biocompatibility, and low toxicity have shown excellent potential for replacing traditional QDs [8–12]. In the field of biomedical researches, GQDs have been exploited as biosensors for biomedical detection assays [13]. Due to quantum confinement effects, GQDs possess interesting features such as high optical quantum yield, narrow fluorescence emission peak, broad excitation spectra, and photo-stability which make them suitable as optical sensors [14]. Thus, design and preparation of optical sensors based on GQDs which have sensitive and selective response toward a specific target is of great interest although a few GQD based selective fluorescence sensors have been reported [15].

One of the suitable approaches for improving the selectivity of QDs based nanosensors is the utilization of molecular imprinted polymer (MIP) technology. This method comprised from designing and fabrication of nanosensors with particular cavities for recognition of desired target analytes [16]. MIPs have some advantages such as high affinity and selectivity, chemical stability, and low cost. Thus, they have been shown as one of the most efficient tools in the field of diagnostics and analysis [17–19]. Recently, fluorescent nanosensors based on integrating QDs and MIPs have shown excellent potential in the recognition and detection of various analytes. Upon inclusion of analyte species in MIP cavities, the fluorescence emission of the QDs changes. This phenomenon may be useful for detection and measurement of various analyte species [20–23]. On the other hand, methamphetamine (METH) and its derivatives are frequently consumed as addictive compounds or recreational drugs rather than medical across the world [24–26]. Thus, development of quick, appropriate, precise, and selective methods for its recognition in trace amounts is crucial. Various methods have been reported for recognition of METH such as gas chromatography (GC) [27], liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [28], GC-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [29], and ion mobility spectrometry (IMS) [30]. Nevertheless, such methods are expensive and need several preparation steps prior to the main procedure.

Here, we report an efficient and simple strategy for METH recognition by utilizing a new nanosensor based on integrating MIP and graphene QDs (GQDs). This approach is low cost, highly selective, and rapid to recognize METH species in solution and does not show response to other analytes such as amphetamine, ibuprofen, codeine, and morphine.
Details of the utilized chemicals and equipments have been given in supplementary information.

First, preparation of GQDs was conducted by pyrolysis of citric acid as described earlier [31]. 2 g (0.01 mol) of citric acid in a 5 mL beaker was heated to 200 °C for about 30 min until an orange liquid appeared. Afterwards, 100 mL of 10 mg.mL− 1 NaOH solution was added to the liquid with continuous stirring. In order to prepare the rGQDs, 0.4 g of NaBH4 was added to 100 mL of the above prepared GQD solution (pH = 14). The pH of mixture was adjusted to 10 by adding HCl (0.1 M) solution. Finally, the solution was heated at 80 °C for 3 h under reflux condition to obtain rGQDs.

100 mL of the rGQDs solution was adjusted to pH = 7 by adding HCl solution (0.1 M). Then, 0.2 mL of triethoxyvinylsilane (TEVS) was added and the mixture stirred for 24 h in room temperature. After addition of ethanol, the vGQDs precipitate was isolated by centrifugation, washed with ethanol to remove the excess of TEVS, and dried at 60 °C overnight.

0.1 g of vGQDs was dispersed in 3 mL of chloroform. Then, 0.01 g of methamphetamine (METH) and 0.08 mL of methacrylic acid (MAA) were added and the mixture stirred for 30 min. After addition of 0.15 mL of ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) and 0.017 g of azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), the mixture was first stirred under N2 atmosphere for 15 min in room temperature and then heated at 60 °C in a water bath for 3 h. After polymerization reaction, the METH template was removed by Soxhlet extraction with methanol and acetic acid (9,1) to achieve GQDs@MIP.
The non-imprinted polymer (GQDs@NIP) was prepared similarly in the absence of the METH template.

The recognition properties of GQDs@MIP were studied by recording the fluorescence spectra in the presence of various METH concentrations at wavelength of 420 nm. The excitation wavelength of 365 nm was used in the spectrofluorometric measurements. 1 mg of GQDs@MIP was dissolved in 3 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) in a quartz cuvette. Then, various concentrations of METH (0–50 μM) were sequentially injected into the cuvette and the fluorescence spectra were recorded after each addition. The controlled experiments of GQDs@NIP were similar to those of GQDs@MIP. Also, some other drugs such as amphetamine, ibuprofen, codeine, and morphine were tested in the same conditions to investigate their potential interference effect.

First, GQDs were prepared and then reduced with NaBH4 in order to convert the carbonyl or carboxy groups to hydroxyl ones and produce the rGQDs [32]. It should be noticed that treatment with NaBH4 allows restoring, at least partially, the aromatic network of rGQDs. In the next step, triethoxyvinylsilane was utilized to functionalize the rGQDs with vinyl groups. The vinylsilyl-modified GQDs (vGQDs) were then polymerized with methacrylic acid as functional monomer and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as cross-linker in the presence of METH as template molecules. Consequently, a polymeric network was formed in which the METH molecules entrapped within the cavities. After removing the METH molecules with soxhlet extraction using a mixture of methanol and acetic acid, the cavity sites were released (Scheme 1 ).

FT-IR spectroscopy provided evidences for the modification of rGQDs and formation of GQDs@MIP. As shown in Fig. 1 , FT-IR spectrum of rGQDs revealed broad peaks around 1394 cm−1 and 1574 cm−1 attributed to COO symmetric and the asymmetric stretching vibrations, respectively. The broad band around 3406 cm−1 is attributed to OH stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups. In the FT-IR spectrum of vGQDs, a broad peak around 1071 cm− 1 can be assigned to the Si-O-C asymmetric stretching vibration indicating the successful silylation of GQDs. The characteristic peak of GQDs@MIP at 1705 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of COO groups of MAA and EGDMA in MIP.

The microstructure of GQDs@MIP was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (Fig. 2 ). Due to the amorphous polymeric nature of MIP particles, irregular shapes were seen in the TEM image. However, The GQDs with size of about 5 nm and spherical morphology can be seen in this image.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption analysis (BET method) was utilized to determine the specific surface area and study the porosity of the GQDs@MIP before and after including the METH molecules (Fig. 2). The BET surface areas of template free GQDs@MIP before and after addition of METH were determined to be 5 m2.g− 1 and 2 m2.g− 1, respectively. As expected, the free GQDs@MIP exhibited higher surface area owing to the presence of empty cavities remained after extracting the primary template molecules.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the GQD, rGQDs, and vGQDs samples were illustrated in Fig. 3 . The GQDs exhibited a peak at 365 nm due to the n-π* transition in CO groups. After reducing GQDs or functionalization by TEVS, no change was seen in the location of the peak, although the absorption intensity of the n-π* transition was reduced due to the reduction of carbonyl groups of GQDs and decreasing n-π* transitions.

Fluorescence spectra of GQDs@MIP were recorded in the presence of various concentrations of METH and the results were shown in Fig. 5a. A linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) and the concentrations of METH in the range of 5–50 μM was observed in the Stern-Volmer plot as given in Fig. 5c. The fluorescence quenching performance can be described by the Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
/ F =
K SV
·
C MET
+ 1
where F0 and F represent the fluorescent intensities in the absence of METH and in a given METH concentration, respectively, CMETH is the concentration of METH, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant [33,34].

The non-imprinted nanocomposite GQDs@NIP did not show obvious change upon addition of METH (Fig. 5b). Compared with the GQDs@NIP, the GQDs@MIP has specific binding sites (within the cavities) suitable for the selective interaction with METH molecules. Thus, GQDs@MIP shows more sensitivity and selectivity for METH determination. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.7 μg/L (12 nM). Hence, the obtained GQDs@MIP could be utilized as a selective sensor for the quantitative analysis of trace amounts of METH.

Comparison of the results of METH determination in the presence of GQDs@MIP with some other methods is listed in Table 1 . In brief, this novel GQDs@MIP exhibited high performance for sensing the METH.

The selectivity of the proposed nanosensor was investigated by comparing the fluorescence response with respect to interfering analogues such as amphetamine (AMPH), ibuprofen (IBU), codeine (COD), and morphine (MORPH) (Fig. 6 ). In the process of GQDs@MIP synthesis, specific recognition sites with respect to the METH template were produced and these sites were complementary in size, shape, and spatial requirements to METH. Thus, METH molecules have superiority in occupying the sites over the other analogues. The template METH molecules could be included in the imprinted sites and cause significant changes in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs.

The Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv) values of METH interaction with GQDs@MIP were higher than other drugs as shown in Fig. 7 . Discrimination in the interaction of GQDs@MIP with METH, AMPH, IBU, COD, and MORPH can be seen in this Figure. The results clearly indicate that the GQDs@MIP has a good selectivity for METH detection. This can be explained by the synthesis process of GQDs@MIP in which specific recognition sites with respect to the METH are produced in the GQDs@MIP. Thus, the METH analyte could be bound strongly to the GQDs@MIP leading to the efficient quenching of the fluorescence intensity.

In summary, a novel nanosensor based on GQDs encapsulated within a MIP was utilized for the selective sensing of METH. This approach integrated the high selectivity of molecular imprinting polymer and strong fluorescence intensity of GQDs. Triethoxyvinylsilane (TEVS) was used for establishing a link between the GQDs and acrylate polymer. Porous structure of MIP after removing the template molecules provided sensitivity and selectivity toward the METH molecules. This optical nanosensor revealed low detection limit and good accuracy in METH analysis which results in developing cheap, sensitive, and selective nanosensors.
Majid Masteri-Farahani: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Shadi Mashhadi-Ramezani: Investigation, Data curation. Nazanin Mosleh: Writing - original draft, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Kharazmi University for the financial support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.118021.

J. Mater. Sci. Mater. Electron.
